skill.name: cirq
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Cirq-based quantum circuit design, simulation, hardware execution, noise modeling, transformations, and experiment workflows.
skill.summary: Build, simulate, optimize, and run Cirq quantum circuits with practical workflow guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk hits, but review found they are documentation examples for Cirq, not executable skill logic. The main residual concerns are low-risk operational guidance for installing packages, authenticating to quantum hardware providers, and using provider endpoints; no prompt injection, hidden command execution, or credential exfiltration intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across many Markdown examples. The reviewed locations are fenced Python or bash documentation examples and are not automatically executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown code fences and consists of Cirq tutorial snippets. There is no wrapper script, hook, or instruction that causes automatic execution.

low_findings.title: Provider Credential Examples Require Care
low_findings.description: Hardware integration examples mention API keys, tokens, and environment variables for IonQ, AQT, Pasqal, and Google Cloud. These are placeholders and setup notes, not embedded credentials, but users should avoid placing real secrets in prompts or generated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use placeholder values such as your_api_key and your_token. The risk is operational secret handling, not evidence of credential theft.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Provider URLs Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill links to official Cirq resources and quantum provider endpoints. These URLs support hardware setup guidance and do not receive hidden data from the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation for known quantum computing services and official references. No code path sends local files, secrets, or prompt data to those endpoints.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Path Traversal Alerts Are Placeholder Paths
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert maps to an Azure resource identifier containing ellipses. It is a placeholder for cloud configuration, not filesystem traversal or file access logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text is an Azure resource_id example in Markdown. It does not build paths, read files, or use user-controlled filesystem input.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Quantum Terminology
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and command-and-control alerts are caused by quantum computing terms, gate names, or variables such as CNOT controls. They are not cryptographic implementations or C2 behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows these sections discuss quantum gates, benchmarking, and decomposition. No encryption routine, remote-control logic, or suspicious networking is present.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alerts Are Static Python Imports
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts point to normal Python import statements inside tutorial snippets. They import Cirq transformer APIs and do not load arbitrary modules from user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports are fixed module names in Markdown examples. There is no JavaScript import expression or runtime module path construction.

content.value_statement: Quantum circuit work needs accurate framework choices, hardware constraints, and noise-aware workflows. This skill provides focused Cirq guidance for simulation, optimization, experiments, and provider execution.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Cirq is a good fit compared with Qiskit, PennyLane, and QuTiP.
Guides construction of qubits, gates, measurements, parameterized circuits, and common circuit patterns.
Supports simulator workflows for state vectors, density matrices, parameter sweeps, noisy circuits, and stabilizer circuits.
Documents hardware integration patterns for Google Quantum AI, IonQ, Azure Quantum, AQT, and Pasqal.
Covers circuit transformations, hardware compilation, gate decomposition, routing, and optimization pipelines.
Provides noise modeling, characterization, error mitigation, and experiment organization guidance.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples but does not execute quantum jobs by itself.
Hardware access still requires valid provider accounts, credentials, quotas, and installed Cirq provider packages.
Examples may need adaptation for the installed Cirq version and current provider APIs.
Large simulations can exceed local memory and may require smaller circuits or specialized infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Quantum Circuit
use_cases.description: Design a small Cirq circuit, add measurements, simulate results, and understand the output before expanding the experiment.

use_cases.title: Prepare Hardware Runs
use_cases.description: Validate device constraints, select qubits, optimize circuits for a target provider, and plan authentication setup.

use_cases.title: Study Noise and Mitigation
use_cases.description: Create realistic noise models, compare noisy and ideal simulation results, and choose mitigation methods for experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Circuit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Cirq skill to help me build a two-qubit Bell state circuit, simulate it, and explain the expected measurement results.

prompt_templates.title: Add Parameter Sweeps
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Cirq skill to design a parameterized circuit with one rotation angle, run a sweep, and describe how to summarize the histogram results.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Hardware
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Cirq skill to review my circuit plan for Google Quantum AI hardware, including qubit selection, validation, and optimization steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Noise Study
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Cirq skill to plan a noise characterization experiment with calibration data, noisy simulation, error mitigation, and result analysis.

output_examples.input: I need a simple Cirq learning example for entanglement.
output_examples.output: A concise Bell state workflow with qubit setup, gate sequence, measurement plan, simulator choice, and interpretation of balanced correlated outcomes.

output_examples.input: I want to run a circuit on IonQ through Cirq.
output_examples.output: A hardware readiness checklist covering package installation, API token handling, simulator testing, target selection, job submission, and result retrieval.

output_examples.input: My noisy simulation does not match ideal results.
output_examples.output: A diagnostic plan comparing noise channels, circuit depth, readout errors, density matrix simulation settings, and mitigation options.

best_practices: Validate every circuit on a simulator before submitting hardware jobs.
Keep provider credentials outside prompts, notebooks, and committed files.
Track circuit depth, qubit mapping, repetitions, noise model, and provider metadata for reproducibility.
anti_patterns: Do not assume a circuit that simulates locally satisfies hardware connectivity or gateset constraints.
Do not paste real API keys or cloud resource secrets into generated examples.
Do not use density matrix simulation for large pure-state circuits unless noise modeling requires it.

faq.question: What is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for Cirq workflows involving Google Quantum AI hardware, circuit simulation, noise modeling, transformations, and quantum experiments.

faq.question: Can it run quantum hardware jobs directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and code patterns. You still need local packages, provider accounts, credentials, and job permissions.

faq.question: Does it support providers beyond Google Quantum AI?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include IonQ, Azure Quantum, AQT, and Pasqal integration patterns through Cirq-related packages.

faq.question: How should I handle API keys?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or provider-managed authentication. Do not paste real keys into prompts, generated code, or repositories.

faq.question: When should I use another quantum framework?
faq.answer: Use Qiskit for IBM hardware, PennyLane for quantum machine learning with autodiff, and QuTiP for physics simulations.

faq.question: Are the examples version-specific?
faq.answer: They may require adjustment for your installed Cirq version and current provider APIs. Check provider documentation before hardware runs.
